nativeRange.habitat: Warm freshwater bodies with soft substrates suitable for burrowing — slow-flowing rivers, marshes, swamps, ditches, reservoirs, irrigation systems, and rice paddies. Tolerates short periods of slightly brackish water and can survive drought periods of several months by sealing itself into burrows.

decorPreferences: deep sand or fine gravel substrate (5+ cm) for burrowing
PVC pipes, caves, and clay flowerpots as hides
no live plants (will be uprooted and consumed)

diet.notes: Generally regarded as an opportunistic omnivore and scavenger. Has been documented consuming sinking pellets, algae wafers, blanched vegetables (zucchini, cucumber, spinach, peas), frozen bloodworms, brine shrimp, mussel and shrimp meat, and small live prey such as snails, worms, and tadpoles. Juveniles tend toward higher-protein carnivorous diets; adults take in a higher proportion of plant matter. Calcium-enriched foods or supplements are commonly recommended to support successful molting. Uneaten food should generally be removed within a few hours to protect water quality.

goodWith: fast-swimming mid- and upper-zone fish kept in tanks much larger than the crayfish's territory
best kept alone in a dedicated species-only tank
avoidWith: other crayfish (highly cannibalistic, especially around molting)
slow or bottom-dwelling fish (catfish, loaches, plecos)
any small or peaceful community fish
live planted tanks (plants will be cut and uprooted)

summary: A large, hardy, highly aggressive freshwater crayfish native to the south-central United States and northern Mexico. Globally one of the most widespread invasive crustaceans; sale and possession are legally restricted in many jurisdictions.
careNotes:
IMPORTANT LEGAL CAVEAT: Procambarus clarkii is on the EU's List of Invasive Alien Species of Union Concern (Regulation 2016/1141, in force 3 August 2016) — import, sale, breeding, transport, and intentional release are prohibited across the European Union. Possession of Red Swamp Crayfish is also illegal or restricted in the United Kingdom, several US states (including Michigan, Washington, and Maine), parts of Canada, Australia, and Japan. Prospective keepers MUST confirm local legality before acquiring this species, and the animal must NEVER be released to the wild — it is one of the most ecologically damaging freshwater invertebrates known, displacing native crayfish, transmitting crayfish-plague (Aphanomyces astaci) to which it is largely immune, and undermining waterway infrastructure through extensive burrowing.

This entry consolidates the species and all commonly-traded color morphs, which share identical husbandry requirements: the wild-type Red form, Orange/Neon Red, Scarlet (dark red), Blue/Ghost Crayfish (sometimes leucistic with red and blue highlights), Yellow/Fireball, White Specter, Tangerine Lobster, and rarer black morphs. Morphs are often sold under marketing names that obscure the underlying species; keepers should treat any orange or blue 'lobster' from a North American crayfish supplier as potentially this species and verify ID before purchase.

Generally a hardy and adaptable species tolerant of a wide temperature range (reported tolerance roughly 8-33°C with a preferred husbandry window around 18-26°C) and pH 6.0-9.0 (preference 6.5-8.0). Hard, mineral-rich water is commonly advised to support exoskeleton formation; soft water is associated with molting failures. A tight-fitting lid with all cable and hose gaps sealed is essential — these crayfish are notorious escape artists and will climb filter intakes, heater cords, and airline tubing to leave the tank. Avoid copper-based medications and snail treatments, which can be lethal. Live plants are not practical with this species (they will be cut, uprooted, and eaten); plastic or silk decor and bare-bottom-plus-substrate setups are usually preferred. Cross-source notes: aquarium care guides cite minimum tank sizes ranging from 20 US gal (~76 L) to 80 L per adult; the value above is biased toward the larger end of cited recommendations and rounded to a nice value, reflecting the species' territorial behavior and ~30 cm claw-spread footprint. Reported adult body lengths range from ~7.5 cm (smaller-source minimum) to ~15 cm (wild maximum); the value above brackets the typical aquarium adult range.
breedingNotes: Generally considered easy to breed in captivity when legal to do so — pairs reliably reproduce in any stable warm tank with adequate burrowing substrate. Females carry 100-500 fertilized eggs attached to the swimmerets ('in berry') for 2-3 weeks until hatching; newly hatched juveniles cling to the mother for several additional weeks before dispersing. The species can complete its life cycle in 4-5 months under favorable conditions and may produce two broods per year. Parthenogenesis has been reported in laboratory settings. Note that successful captive breeding is precisely why this species has become so invasive globally; breeders are legally responsible for ensuring no offspring escape to local waterways, and breeding is explicitly prohibited under EU and several national regulations.

primary.name: Aquarium Co-Op
primary.notes: Designated primary returned no dedicated Procambarus clarkii care article; Aquarium Co-Op coverage is limited to forum threads and a freshwater dwarf shrimp blog post that briefly mentions Procambarus juvenile protein needs. Substituted with Aquariumbreeder.com (Shrimp and Snail Breeder) as functional primary care reference.

additional.name: Shrimp and Snail Breeder (aquariumbreeder.com)
additional.notes: Functional primary source. Provided adult size 10-12 cm (15 cm wild), lifespan up to 3-5 years, preferred temperature 22-27°C with tolerance 8-33°C, pH 7.0-8.0 (tolerance 6.0-9.0), gH 6-8 (tolerance 3-22), kH 4-6 (tolerance 2-18), 20 US-gal/~90 L minimum tank per adult, aggressive territorial temperament, omnivore diet, easy breeding, family Cambaridae, color morphs (red/orange/scarlet/blue/yellow/white), invasive status.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping
additional.notes: Designated secondary direct page returned HTTP 403; content recovered via web search excerpt. Confirmed UK illegality of Procambarus clarkii (often sold as 'red crayfish'/'red lobster'), Salmon and Freshwater Fisheries Act 1975 prohibition on keeping non-native crayfish except Cherax quadricarinatus.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Procambarus clarkii)
additional.notes: Taxonomy and range fallback (Wiki direct access blocked; data recovered via search). Confirmed family Cambaridae, native range from northern Mexico through Gulf states to Florida Panhandle and inland to southern Illinois via Mississippi Basin, adult length 5.5-12 cm, average lifespan 5 years (some >6), EU Union List inclusion since 14 July 2016 (Regulation 2016/1141), color morphs including white/blue/orange in the pet trade.

additional.name: AnimalSpot - Procambarus clarkii
additional.notes: Additional cross-check. Confirmed family Cambaridae, order Decapoda, adult size 7.6-12.7 cm, lifespan 3-5 years, max comfortable temperature ~28°C, nocturnal burrowing behavior, omnivorous diet, late-autumn breeding, IUCN Least Concern but globally most-introduced crayfish.

additional.name: Caring For Your Crayfish Buddies - Red Swamp Crayfish
additional.notes: Additional husbandry cross-check. Confirmed minimum 20 US-gal tank size, aggressive temperament, omnivorous diet, Ghost color morph commonly traded, molting behavior (pre-molt hiding, post-molt vulnerability), no-release imperative.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Generally molts every few days as a hatchling, every 1-2 weeks as a juvenile, and roughly every 4-8 weeks as an adult; intervals are commonly reported to lengthen with age and shorten at higher temperatures. Crayfish typically retreat into a burrow or hide before molting, refuse food for several days, and are highly vulnerable for 1-2 days post-molt while the new exoskeleton hardens. The discarded exoskeleton should be left in the tank for re-consumption (calcium source). Successful molting requires adequate gH and dissolved calcium; soft water is widely associated with incomplete-molt mortality and limb loss.
crustacean.breedingNotes: See top-level breedingNotes. Easy to breed where legal; broods of 100-500 eggs and rapid generational turnover are precisely what make this species such an aggressive invader once released.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: On the EU List of Invasive Alien Species of Union Concern since 2016 (Regulation 2016/1141) — sale, import, breeding, and possession are prohibited across the EU. Also illegal or restricted in the UK, several US states, parts of Canada, Australia, and Japan. Verify local law before considering this species.
